text: Hey, guys. Holofan4life here. Welcome to the Familiar of Zero 20th anniversary rewatch. Tsundere fans rejoice! This is the second part of a three part series involving rewatches of shows with Rie Kugimiya as the main female heroine. The first part was Shakugan no Shana, and the last will be Hayate the Combat Butler with a rewatch in 2027. For those who remember, I did a rewatch of Familiar of Zero in 2023. That was really when I started to dip my toe in the water in terms of doing rewatches for a full time basis. And I remember the show better better than its reputation suggests. I consider Familiar of Zero to be my guilty pleasure anime. It's not perfect, but it does a lot of stuff good. Also, it features a good mix of action, romance, comedy, drama, and Rie Kugimiya at arguably her most helplessly adorable. What's not to like? We'll get to it... I'm entirely doing this rewatch because I love tsunderes. And as extreme as the show can be at times, I have a massive soft spot for Louise and Saito and the world this show embodies. With that out of the way, let’s begin. I'm watching the sub, by the way. The back of my throat is so scratchy. I hope I'm not sick. If I don't get a job by tomorrow, I'mma lose it. Louise in a grassy field She sees Tabitha and Saito being close to each other. But Louise can't use her explosion magic. I can hear Rie Kugimiya say the word Explosion in English all day long. Can't use any magic And then Louise wakes up Well, that explains that weird screen
label: hot_take

text: Sadly it's never the "I love you"s or kisses that you remember. You remember that time you fought, all the times you've been at each others throats, that one time he leaned in to kiss someone else right in front of you (which is still his worst offence BY FAR). And you start to spiral and wonder if everything he said was a lie and if you're worthless like you always thought. And yet you can't bring yourself to break up because if you do you have absolutely nothing left What Holo did for you then is what Guitar-Bocchi did for me. Leaving your comfort zone and putting yourself out there is one of the bravest things you can do and I commend everyone that does it because I'm terrified of it. When BtR came out with this socially anxious mess of a main character that can barely hold a conversation, I finally felt seen in a way I never had before. And it got so absurdly popular out of nowhere that a lot of people must've felt the same way. I try to put myself out there at least a little more these days and BtR is no small part of the reason for that, but sometimes I still just don't wanna talk to anyone for a week
label: analysis

text: One last thing I want to make note of is the portrayal of loneliness in both Louise and Henrietta. They each crave companionship, but they handle it differently where Henrietta is almost like destroying those she cares about and Louise is destroying herself. I think Henrietta is almost this reflection of Louise if she handled things terribly and went about her loneliness in a selfish matter. No one deserves to be lonely, but that doesn't mean others have to suffer because you want to try to course correct.

text: Agreed, it's sitting at a solid 7 right now with the (high) potential to go up. I suppose that 'something missing' for me is probably a clearer goal? Right now we hardly know what everyone wants to achieve, and the MC only has the vaguely defined goal of finding his parents with no concrete plan on how to do so. So it feels like we're getting worldbuilding and lore stuff for the sake of it. Good worldbuilding mind you, but still feels a tad bit meandering. Agreed on the more showing stuff, but to the show's credit it at least tries to imply more than what the characters talked about, which is more than can be said for most shonen(?) shows. E.g. the villagers in the last episode casually talking about Seal, implying they had the motivation and probably a hand in keep the MC oblivious and trapped in the village.
label: hot_take

text: Sharp and tight is exactly how I'd describe the show, too. It hasn't (yet) reached that "excellent" status for me by any means, as there's something missing in it that I can't put my finger on, but I can't help but really, really like how the characters are written and how the narrative feels multi-dimensional. And sure, it's often a lot of just talking and talking which causes the show to suffer since it's a visual medium (this isn't me saying there should be more battles, by the way, just wishing they utilized more "showing" than "tell") but the writing is just that good that I get sucked into it and the episode passes by before I realize. Everyone's got hidden motives and there's something new to learn about a character or the greater narrative every episode. I'm just waiting for the ball to drop at this point and see the world lore or daemon stuff to open things up beyond this rival clan narrative. It'll probably happen when Yuru finally gets Seal.
label: analysis

text: Yeah, \[spoilers for the first few episodes\] at some point it becomes clear that episode 1 was done purely for shock value, which is so bad because it makes the subsequent writing feel really dumb. The protagonist's entire village is massacred in a sadistic way, then before anyone comes to terms with that, the perpetrators start to get portrayed as all cute and cuddly, with Yuru joining them and barely asking any questions about the massacre. That for me was enough to drop the series. I'm sure later on there was going to be a reveal that actually the villagers were the true bad guys or whatever, but if that comes to pass, it's a very silly and predictable setup, but more importantly, it doesn't explain how Yuru was so chill around these people. I also *heavily* dislike the idea of an entire village being portrayed as collectively guilty of a crime worthy of mass execution (if that's where the story was going.) That's the kind of thing that makes me write-off someone as an author and start to question their motivations. In hindsight I think if they did episode 1 differently and didn't go hard on the edgelord shock value I probably could have stayed with this show a bit more just to see how they were going to justify things. But even then the chances of it being convincing was still gonna be very low.
label: analysis

text: **Part 2** Overall, I thought this was a satisfying way to end the season. It was like Louise's crowning achievement as a character after so many L's taken and her emotions getting the better of her. The action was great and easily the most exciting since the Menville arc, and Saito and Louise had a couple heartfelt moments that gave the episode weight. I'd like to think this will officially mark a change in their relationship, but with nothing official it's hard to say. I do think it was clever to have the episode be about Louise worrying about losing her Void mage powers. That made for a good parallel to the beginning of the season which was about Saito losing his Gandalfr powers. A lot of interesting directions were set up in this episode. Louise is now officially someone of importance, and Joseph and Sheffield are still around causing chaos. It feels like they will be the main antagonists heading into the series finale. I could see a situation where with Louise and Tabitha now in prominent positions of power, the war from season 2 gets resumed and Tristain takes a more assertive approach. To let Joseph carry on when he's committed crimes against humanity seems a bit like turning a blind eye. It also feels like the end of Tabitha's character arc, and I thought that was handled well. It was one of the better parts of season 3 and helped prevent the season from being a total waste. I wonder if we'll see Tabitha bond with her mother now that she's awake and seems to have gain
label: analysis

text: I just think that's a rather cynical way of looking at it, especially coming from an author who wrote a similar dynamic for the war arc of their other popular work that needn't be named. The story doesn't go "actually the village was bad, so it's fine." It shows why the Kagemori think the village is bad, and how they justify their indiscriminate murder of the people there. This is another case of viewers thinking what the characters are thinking is endorsement from the authors side rather than just what is being presented, and it's kind of frustrating. I feel like that's where "trust in the viewer" comes in. Obviously no one is going to turn to the camera and say "that was fucked up Gabby" because she's surrounded by people who endorsed and coordinated the attack. Yuru has expressed his anger with Gabby's actions already, and that's about all we'll get. Instead we get scenes like in today's episode where we get a follow-up on the kid who she traumatized for life by murdering her mother in front of her so we as the viewers can make up our own minds on her character and actions. We see the result of her killings affecting people who weren't even apart of the conflict she's taking part in. This is trust in the viewer, this is subtlety in writing that doesn't want to tell you how to think. I just think anime is so unused to this kind of writing that people don't know how to grapple with it sometimes.
label: analysis

text: buddy i have news for you LOL Tabitha is so goated she can't even take an L for a minute Louise is SO GOD DAMN CUTE It's really interesting to see how you're annoyed at the cast for not caring about Louise, and then others get annoyed at Louise herself. NGL with like 3-4 rewatches of this season under my belt and going in with a more open and critical eye, i ended up liking it better than i did This is more in line with what I expected everyone to react this season, it felt meandering and fluffy and fanservice-y, but on this rewatch I can appreciate Louise learning to trust Saito more even when it's difficult, the Agnes Colbert saga, and my favourite part of the season when Saito loses his rune, challenging the idea of master and familiar when they don't have that bond. Though it does kinda retread that idea with Gandalf being the reason why Saito loves Louise again in the last episode, I do overall think the season is much better than I remembered

text: ANSWER TODAY’S QUESTION(S) What are your thoughts on Louise losing the ability to use magic? Interesting, first Saito lost his powers, now it's Louise's turn, but this plot twist is still too sudden. What are your thoughts on Louise wondering if Saito's love for her is because he's under the influence of Gandalfr? A lack of security that is unique to girls in the dating phase？[](#sakurathink) What are your thoughts on Louise regaining her Void powers because Tabitha kissed Saito? She suffered a great shock. What are your thoughts on Henrietta declaring Louise now her sister and second successor to the throne? She is truly magnanimous. Bonus) How would you compare this season to the previous two? The other girls were lovely, especially Siesta in the latter half. Louise's experience of Plebian life and the themes of war and hatred were already well explored in the S1 and S2; this season felt like an unnecessary addition. Bonus 2) What are your thoughts on Louise as it currently stands? Her mental state is becoming increasingly unstable; I hope Saito can protect her. Bonus 3) After three seasons, what do you make of Saito and Louise and their relationship as a whole? They have a somewhat symbiotic relationship; whoever leaves first, the remaining people will be at a loss. Bonus 4) What are you hoping we see in season 4? A slightly more in-depth content like S2
label: hot_take

text: **First Timer** Back from watching Disclosure Day, now it’s time to talk about Familiar of Zero Okay maybe I’m just blanking out on how the last episode ended but where is everyone and why are they surrounded by fog? Also why did Louise wake up on the ground? I see, just a dream fakeout. I guess everyone’s just escaped and is lying low? I guess Louise used up all her magic defeating that elf last episode? Not sure what’s going on with her now. Are we implying that Tabitha has a thing for Saito now? I’m not sure I like this development. I’m kinda sick of it seeming like every woman he meets has a thing for him. The way Joseph says this line about killing his brother makes it seem like it’s a big reveal but I feel like we already knew this, didn’t we? Oh hey, Julio’s in this episode. He’s done basically nothing all season. Saito is starting to learn the local language of this world. I’m surprised it’s taken him this long. Louise seems to be freaking out a bit. Jormungand is here. He looks very intimidating. Also, very distinctly not a snake despite his namesake. There’s some pretty good animation in this fight like when Saito’s rolling down the hill. It’s cool seeing the Ostland show up for the battle here. It’s lovely seeing Saito save Louise here, very dramatic. I really like Saito hugging Louise from behind. It’s very sweet. Man, how does old military equipment just keep ending up in this world? I was fully expecting the artillery shell to take down Jormungand. Not sure what
label: analysis

text: Rewatcher. You know, I don't think I'd fully appreciated just how many moving parts this show has, because of how well it manages them all. DA's operations, Shinji and the Alan institute, Majima and his plans, Chisato's heart, Chisato's past, Mika's past (with Shinji), Takina's character arc, and Chisato and Takina's relationship--every single one of these pieces just gets one more little nugget of development at a time in a way that's really well-handled. I say that because I remember not initially thinking quite as much of this episode because the development of Takina and Chisato's relationship takes more of a backseat, but what we got instead is doing a very efficient job of moving the plot forward. I don't think I have much to say that others aren't going to be echoing. Learning about Chisato's heart, showing that Shinji and the Alan institute are no longer going to be passive about Chisato delivering her gift to the world, and my lord so much thematic foreshadowing. Kurumi shows quite a bit more of her sociopathic side this episode. Laughing at the TV broadcast, smiling at the idea of Mizuki having sacrificed herself. What a little weirdo. "I worked with Silent Jin for years, but I don't think I ever heard his voice." [Immediately upon capture] "Oh hey Mika, how's it going?" In subs, Jin asks Mika what happened to his leg, while in ENG dub, he asks "how's your leg?" [Last episode vague spoiler] >! This little discrepancy actually gets very interesting, when you consider
label: analysis

If she can heal herself, reviving someone who's near death is definitely doable. What does this mean for Ayame tho? Does this mean she's now the new Agent of Summer? Or would it go back to Ruri now that she's been revived? If not, then the role reversal between sisters would be actually pretty funny.

text: Like I like the first 2/3rds of this season despite it apparently being weaker than the last 3rd ( Which, obviously, I disagree.). My issue is purely with the last 3rd on top of foundational issues the show's always had (I've been complaining about Louise not growing as a character. This isn't new.). I don't think dragging out a plot point that was ultimately meaningless (The familiar pact being broken.) and cutting out the fun stuff (the comedy) would fix the season and in fact feel it would make it worse. Like. Louise standing up to Henrietta was never going to work because the show hasn't put in the work to make it work. She's always happily offered to sacrifice herself for Henrietta or to do whatever Henrietta asks of her. There's been no hints that she might eventually snap. And frankly with how much stake she puts in her aristocrat title and her feelings on honor throwing that away for Tabitha feels out of character. Like I could maybe see her do it for Saito and even then that's questionable. And then there's Kirche. Kirche has barely been around after the first season. When would she have been given reason to start relying on Louise? Like unless they seeded the idea throughout the show prior it was never going to feel earned. And they didn't do that because they couldn't since she was barely around last season and didn't really get much focus this season before the last 3rd. The Agnes - Colbert-sensei storyline is maybe the only one of the three that works and even th
label: analysis

text: **Rewatcher** So Shinji kidnapped a dying patient from a hospital and hired an assassin to be killed by Chisato. Surely he has a good reason to do so, surely... But he had no idea she was so much against the very idea of killing. And we also now know why. Her heart is artificial, and that is what she was gifted by the Alan Institute. Funnily, or tragically, that is what made her want to not take any more lives. And if the idea behind giving her that heart was making her execute her talent of killing people, there is some heavy conflict coming her way. Loved the action sequence this episode as well. In my memory, Takina did worse here and I didn't like that she had to be rescued, but she was doing fine. She only took a strafing hit and might have handled the situation on her own, but it was still good Chisato came to her aid. Unleashing the rubber bullets at point blank into Jin was a very cathartic finish. And even Mizuki had a chance to shine here. Also Kurumi has by now been fully adopted into the crew, which is nice. I also loved the little bit of panic in Takina's voice when she first heard about Chisato's artificial heart. She genuinely cares about Chisato now and is concerned for her, and Shion Wakayama's voice acting really shows off the layers in Takina's character. The little revelation that the police officer knew something about incidents getting covered up is great. As powerful as DA is, they cannot hide everything, particularly not from the police. Finally, Majim
label: analysis
